network.overall_strategy: The network is centered on literature-supported abiotic routes that robustly generate racemic proline or direct proline precursors, then channels those products into a final explicitly low-confidence L-enrichment node because the supplied literature states that no demonstrated prebiotic aqueous route to homochiral L-proline is known. To avoid unsupported compression, the network uses the strongest documented branches: (i) Patel et al. cyanosulfidic photochemistry through a 4-carbon cyanohydrin, cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamide, gamma-butyrothiolactam, and proline aminonitrile; (ii) alpha-ketoglutarate-centered protometabolic chemistry leading to glutamate, glutamic-gamma-semialdehyde, and spontaneous P5C cyclization; (iii) HCN oligomerization through DAMN and polymer/hydantoin-like hydrolysis; and (iv) direct Miller-Urey-type spark discharge occurrence of proline and glutamate in complex mixtures. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies alpha-ketoglutarate and reductive conditions; surface chemistry supplies HCN-rich photochemical and discharge routes; biochemical/prebiotic chemistry assembles, hydrolyzes, cyclizes, and concentrates products. The target designation as L-proline is therefore achieved only via a speculative enrichment step constrained by the literature discussion of eutectic amplification and unresolved chirality.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal protometabolic generation of alpha-ketoglutarate from CO2-fed iron-sulfur networks

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11, serpentinization-derived H2; network-level protometabolic sequence rather than a single experimentally isolated elementary step

reactions.mechanism: Iron-sulfur-driven CO2 fixation and protometabolic carbon-network transformations produce TCA-like intermediates including alpha-ketoglutarate.
reactions.reasoning: Alpha-ketoglutarate is identified in protometabolic TCA-like chemistry and as the key branch point for proline-family synthesis. Pyrite-driven and iron-sulfur-mediated generation of TCA intermediates is supported in hydrothermal contexts, although this node necessarily compresses a multi-step network into a graph connection rather than claiming a single direct elementary reaction. This follows the literature emphasis on hydrothermal protometabolism while avoiding unsupported direct one-step carbon accounting claims (Muchowska et al., 2017; Stubbs et al., 2020; Springsteen et al., 2018; Kelley et al., 2001; Russell & Hall, 1997, 2006).

reactions.name: Nickel-catalyzed reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate

conditions.catalyst: Ni-bearing catalyst with FeS-like hydrothermal mineral context
conditions.other: mild alkaline to near-neutral conditions; graph encoded with hydrothermal iron-sulfur minerals to satisfy vent context while grounded in Ni-catalyzed reductive amination literature

reactions.mechanism: Alpha-keto acid undergoes mineral-assisted reductive amination with ammonia under reducing conditions to yield glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids is directly supported in prebiotic mineral catalysis and recent nickel-catalyzed Krebs-cycle analog chemistry yielding glutamate from alpha-ketoglutarate under H2/NH3. FeS/NiFe sulfide work also supports alpha-keto acid amination in hydrothermal settings (Huber & Wachtershäuser, 1998, 2003; Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Selective partial reduction of glutamate to glutamic-gamma-semialdehyde

conditions.other: alkaline reducing vent fluids; chemically difficult and included as a low-confidence necessary connection

reactions.mechanism: Partial reduction of the gamma-carboxyl group of glutamate gives the corresponding semialdehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied literature explicitly identifies glutamate-to-GSA as the key unresolved bottleneck in the glutamate-family route. It is therefore included as a speculative but necessary network edge, with plausible Fe2+/FeS/H2 reducing conditions noted in the review, and clearly marked as not directly demonstrated with high confidence under prebiotic conditions (Vogel & Davis, 1952; Strecker, 1957; Huber & Wachtershäuser, 1998; review summary in provided findings).

reactions.name: Spontaneous cyclization of glutamic-gamma-semialdehyde to P5C
reactions.mechanism: Intramolecular condensation of the amino aldehyde forms the five-membered imine ring of Delta1-pyrroline-5-carboxylic acid.
reactions.reasoning: This is one of the strongest steps in the entire network: GSA spontaneously cyclizes nonenzymatically in water to P5C, creating the pyrrolidine framework precursor without enzymatic assistance (Vogel & Davis, 1952).

reactions.name: Reduction of P5C to racemic proline

conditions.other: Fe2+, FeS/H2S, or H2-rich reducing milieu plausible; exact prebiotic reducing system unresolved

reactions.mechanism: Reduction of the cyclic imine/carboxylate intermediate yields proline.
reactions.reasoning: The literature review identifies Fe2+, FeS/H2S, or molecular hydrogen from serpentinization as plausible reductants for the P5C-to-proline conversion, though a dedicated prebiotic experimental demonstration remains limited. The step is retained because it is the canonical completion of the glutamate-family route and less problematic than the preceding selective GSA-forming step (Strecker, 1957; review summary in provided findings).

reactions.name: Surface cyanosulfidic formation of 4-carbon cyanohydrin precursor

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox with phosphate buffer; mineral support by sulfide and phosphate minerals
conditions.other: anoxic, neutral pH, UV irradiation; shallow surface pools

reactions.mechanism: HCN-based cyanosulfidic photochemistry generates reduced C4 nitrile/oxygenated intermediates that feed proline synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. describe proline arising from a 4-carbon cyanohydrin within the broader cyanosulfidic network under 35C, neutral pH, UV, H2S, and Cu photoredox cycling. Because the supplied schema requires mineral agents on surface reactions, sulfide and phosphate minerals are included as geochemically plausible supports for the documented H2S/Cu/phosphate system, while preserving the actual reported solution conditions (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic conversion of 4-carbon cyanohydrin to cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamide
reactions.mechanism: Sulfur incorporation into the C4 cyanohydrin system yields open-chain and cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamide species, including the ring-competent cyclic intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: This directly follows the specific proline branch designated as movement c in the cyanosulfidic network, where a 4-carbon cyanohydrin reacts with H2S to give open-chain and cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamides (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: UV-driven cyclization and deoxygenation to gamma-butyrothiolactam

conditions.catalyst: UV light; geochemical photocatalytic support by TiO2

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical rearrangement, ring closure, and deoxygenation convert the cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamide into gamma-butyrothiolactam.
reactions.reasoning: The review specifically states that UV irradiation drives cyclization and deoxygenation to gamma-butyrothiolactam in the Sutherland network. TiO2 is included as a permissible surface photocatalytic mineral under the schema, while the core evidence is the UV-driven transformation documented by Patel and co-workers (Patel et al., 2015; Saladino et al., 2017 for TiO2 relevance).

reactions.name: Photoreduction and HCN addition to form proline aminonitrile

conditions.catalyst: UV photoredox with Cu(I)/Cu(II) network context

reactions.mechanism: Gamma-butyrothiolactam undergoes photoreduction and cyanide incorporation to generate the aminonitrile precursor of proline.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. identify gamma-butyrothiolactam as the immediate precursor that after photoreduction and HCN addition yields the alpha-aminonitrile of proline (compound 47 in the original paper) (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of proline aminonitrile to racemic proline

conditions.other: hydrolytic workup in prebiotic pool or interface

reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile hydrolysis converts the nitrile-bearing precursor into proline.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of aminonitriles to amino acids is the expected completion of Strecker-like and cyanosulfidic amino acid synthesis. The review explicitly states that hydrolysis of the proline aminonitrile yields proline itself (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: HCN tetramerization to DAMN
reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes under concentrated prebiotic conditions to the tetramer diaminomaleonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: DAMN is the canonical HCN tetramer and a key branch point in HCN oligomer chemistry dating back to Oró's work. Mineral concentration on clays and UV-active surfaces is a geochemically plausible encoding under the required schema, while the chemistry itself is broadly supported by HCN oligomerization literature (Oró, 1961; Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Conversion of DAMN into hydantoin-rich HCN polymer mixture

conditions.catalyst: mineral surface concentration with thermal cycling or UV exposure
conditions.other: higher-temperature hydrolysis/aging conditions favor amino-acid-releasing polymers

reactions.mechanism: Further oligomerization, rearrangement, and partial hydrolysis of HCN tetramers produce complex polymeric and hydantoin-like intermediates.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied literature emphasizes that HCN polymer hydrolysis routes to proline are complex and proceed through polymeric and hydantoin-like chemistry rather than a simple direct DAMN-to-proline conversion. This node deliberately preserves that intermediate complexity instead of overcompressing the branch (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN polymer/hydantoin mixture to racemic proline-containing amino acid pool

conditions.other: condition-sensitive mixed-product hydrolysis; proline is a minor component

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic release from nitrile-rich polymer and hydantoin-like precursors yields amino acids including proline.
reactions.reasoning: Proline is reported among amino acids detected in some HCN polymer hydrolysis experiments, particularly at higher temperatures. This is included as a low-specificity but literature-supported auxiliary route and is intentionally not represented as selective synthesis (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Spark discharge generation of HCN from CH4/N2/H2O/NH3 atmospheres

conditions.temperature: electrical discharge plasma with cooling condensate
conditions.catalyst: electric discharge; mineral surfaces act as depositional/concentrating supports
conditions.other: anoxic atmospheric simulation with trace NH3

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge through reduced atmospheric gases generates HCN and other nitriles that rain into surface waters.
reactions.reasoning: HCN is a classic product of Miller-Urey-type discharge chemistry and a realistic feedstock for downstream surface pathways. The feedstock description follows the literature rather than the oversimplified NH3-only representations criticized previously. Mineral agents are included to satisfy schema constraints as plausible receiving and concentrating surfaces rather than claimed essential catalysts of the discharge itself (Ring et al., 1972; Johnson et al., 2008; Jiang et al., 2014).

reactions.name: Spark discharge formation of glutamate in complex amino acid mixtures

conditions.temperature: electrical discharge plasma with aqueous trapping
conditions.other: reduced gas mixture, product analysis by chromatography/MS

reactions.mechanism: Spark discharge produces a complex mixture of amino acids including glutamate-related species in aqueous condensates.
reactions.reasoning: Glutamic acid is reported among amino acids in Miller-type experiments and meteorites, making it a justified surface-source input into the shared glutamate hub rather than a dedicated single-product route. This supports environmental interconnection without inventing unreported selectivity (Ring et al., 1972; Johnson et al., 2008; Kvenvolden et al., 1970; Cronin & Moore, 1971).

reactions.name: Spark discharge direct formation of racemic proline in complex amino acid mixtures

conditions.temperature: electrical discharge plasma with aqueous trapping
conditions.other: reduced to weakly reduced atmospheres; proline likely underreported by OPA/NAC analytical methods

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge chemistry produces diverse amino acids, including proline, as minor components of the resulting mixture.
reactions.reasoning: Direct abiotic occurrence of proline in spark discharge mixtures is firmly documented, including classic identification by ion-exchange/GC-MS and later LC-MS/MS reanalysis. The literature also notes secondary-amine analytical underreporting, reinforcing proline as a valid source term to the racemic proline pool (Ring et al., 1972; Johnson et al., 2008; Jiang et al., 2014; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Speculative prebiotic eutectic/chiral selection from racemic proline to L-proline

conditions.other: crystal-solution partitioning and repeated dissolution/precipitation; prebiotically unresolved because demonstrated strongest in CHCl3/EtOH rather than water

reactions.mechanism: Partial chiral amplification by preferential retention of racemic crystals and enrichment of one enantiomer in solution.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied literature is explicit that no robust prebiotic aqueous route to homochiral L-proline is demonstrated. This reaction is therefore intentionally marked speculative and represents only a possible enrichment/selection process inspired by crystal-solution eutectic amplification, not a validated synthesis step. It is included solely because the requested target is L-proline. The best experimental analogue is the non-prebiotic solvent eutectic amplification of proline ee (Hayashi et al., 2006; Klussmann et al., 2006; review summary in provided findings).

pathways.name: Hydrothermal alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate-family route
pathways.description: Vent protometabolic chemistry produces alpha-ketoglutarate, which is reductively aminated to glutamate, then converted through GSA and spontaneous P5C cyclization to racemic proline before speculative L-enrichment.

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic proline route
pathways.description: Surface HCN/H2S/UV chemistry forms a 4-carbon cyanohydrin, cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamide, gamma-butyrothiolactam, and proline aminonitrile, then hydrolysis gives proline and speculative later L-selection.

pathways.name: Spark-fed cyanosulfidic route
pathways.description: Atmospheric discharge chemistry supplies HCN that feeds the Patel-Sutherland surface branch to racemic proline, then speculative enrichment to L-proline.

pathways.name: HCN oligomerization and polymer hydrolysis route
pathways.description: HCN oligomerizes to DAMN and then into hydantoin-rich polymeric material whose hydrolysis releases proline as a minor product, followed by speculative L-selection.

pathways.name: Spark-fed HCN oligomer route
pathways.description: Spark discharge produces HCN, which then enters DAMN/polymer chemistry and eventually hydrolyzes to a racemic proline pool.

pathways.name: Direct Miller-Urey proline occurrence route
pathways.description: Spark discharge directly generates racemic proline in a complex amino acid mixture, with only speculative downstream L-enrichment.

pathways.name: Spark glutamate into glutamate-family route
pathways.description: Surface spark chemistry generates glutamate that then enters the shared glutamate-to-P5C-to-proline branch.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal alpha-ketoglutarate with spark-supplied glutamate convergence route
pathways.description: Two environments converge at the glutamate hub: hydrothermal alpha-ketoglutarate chemistry and surface spark amino acid synthesis, then shared downstream conversion to proline.

pathways.name: HCN convergence on proline pool from cyanosulfidic and oligomer branches
pathways.description: HCN acts as a central surface hub feeding both the mechanistically strong cyanosulfidic branch and the broader oligomer/polymer hydrolysis branch before convergence at racemic proline.

pathways.name: Whole-network multi-source convergence to L-proline target node
pathways.description: Multiple independent literature-supported routes produce racemic proline or shared precursors; these converge into a common proline pool from which only speculative chiral enrichment can yield the explicit target L-proline.
